topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The memo sat on the worn oak desk like a dead weight. Sarah traced the edges of the printed notice with a trembling finger, the paper crinkling under the pressure. "Closure effective in six weeks," the bold lettering screamed. Her breath hitched, catching somewhere deep in her chest. She stood up abruptly, knocking her chair backward with a loud scrape that echoed in the cavernous, suddenly silent lecture hall. Every shadow seemed to deepen, stretching into menacing shapes along the periphery of the fluorescent lights. A coldness washed over her, seeping from the soles of her sneakers. She backed away slowly, her eyes darting toward the imposing brick facade of the university building, a knot tightening painfully in her stomach. She needed to move, to escape the suffocating certainty of it all.

[story 2]

"This can't be real," Mark mumbled, staring blankly at the email glowing on his laptop screen. The words—'Campus restructuring and eventual cessation of operations'—refused to settle. He reread the sentence three times, his knuckles white where he gripped the keyboard. A sudden, dizzying wave of nausea rolled over him, making the room tilt slightly. He pushed his chair back, the sound unnaturally loud in the quiet dormitory room. His hands began to shake uncontrollably, and he rubbed his temples, trying to force his thoughts into a coherent sequence, but they dissolved into a swirling vortex of panic. He felt trapped, suddenly marooned on a shifting sandbar.

[story 3]

Eleanor kept replaying the final board meeting in her mind, the hushed tones and the vague assurances that felt hollow now. When the official notification arrived via campus-wide announcement, she didn't move for a full minute. Her gaze was fixed on a dust mote dancing in a sunbeam slicing through the window. A sharp, metallic taste flooded her mouth. She walked to the window, pressing her forehead against the cool glass, trying to anchor herself to the solid reality of the outside world. The sounds of the distant campus—the muffled chatter, the occasional passing car—seemed alien, mocking her stillness. Her entire body was rigid, anticipating some catastrophic event that she couldn't see, only feel vibrating beneath her skin.
